text: Cross-examine
Interrogate
Probe
Clarify
Rephrase
Impeach
Concede
Evasive
Leading question
Credibility
Inconsistency
Badger
Could you tell me where you were that night?
Can you tell me whether you saw him?
Do you know what happened?
I would like to know why she left.
Luiz, how will you open your cross-examination tomorrow?
I will ask indirectly first. Could you tell me where you were at nine o'clock? Nothing aggressive.
And if the witness becomes evasive?
Then I rephrase the question and probe one fact at a time. I never badger her.
Do you plan to impeach her credibility?
Only if I find an inconsistency between her statement and her answer today.
What if she refuses to clarify?
I ask the judge whether the witness may be directed to answer. Calm, but firm.

Good morning. Before the witness returns, let me remind counsel how cross-examination works in this court. You may not ask leading questions of your own witness, but on cross-examination a leading question is allowed. When you want information, ask indirectly and politely: could you tell me where you were that evening, or do you know whether the door was locked. When you challenge the witness, be direct, but never badger. If an answer is unclear, ask the witness to clarify rather than to argue. Probe one fact at a time. If you find an inconsistency between today's answer and the earlier statement, point it out calmly and let the court draw its own conclusion. That is how credibility is tested.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. In court today, the defense conducted a careful cross-examination of the key witness. Rather than attacking, the lawyer asked indirectly whether the witness had been certain about the time. The witness grew evasive, so counsel rephrased the question more simply. When asked if she could explain the gap in her account, she finally conceded that she had not been wearing her glasses. The lawyer did not badger her; he simply probed the inconsistency until her credibility was in doubt. Reporters were told that a ruling is expected on Thursday. We will follow the case as it develops.
